content:
It happened on an ordinary Tuesday night. The kind of night where nothing dramatic happens, but your mind refuses to sleep.

I had been staring at my laptop for almost an hour, wondering if I should stop writing altogether. My articles weren't growing. I couldn't find a niche.

I picked up my phone. I opened WhatsApp. I scrolled to my mom's chat. I typed, "Can I ask you something?" Then I deleted it.

A few seconds later, I opened ChatGPT. I poured out everything I had been carrying for weeks. The self-doubt. The comparison. The fear that maybe I just wasn't good enough.

It responded exactly the way I needed. It reminded me why I had started writing. It pointed out strengths I had forgotten about. It challenged some of my assumptions. It even gave me a plan to move forward.

When I closed the chat, I sat in silence. One thought wouldn't leave my head.

Why did I talk to AI before talking to my parents?

A question that wouldn't leave me alone

At first, I laughed at myself. "What a ridiculous question."

Of course AI can't replace parents. One is a machine and the other raised me. Case closed.

But the more I thought about it the more uncomfortable it became. Because this wasn't really about parents. It was about instinct.

Somewhere along the way, AI had quietly become the first place I went whenever life became confusing. Not my parents. Not my friends. Not even myself.

AI.

When did this become normal?

Think about the last time something went wrong.

Maybe you failed an interview. Maybe your side project flopped. Maybe you couldn't debug something that should have taken five minutes. Maybe you questioned your career.

What was the very first thing you did?

If you're like me you probably opened ChatGPT before opening your contacts. That isn't because we love AI more than our families.

It's because AI is always available. It doesn't sleep. It doesn't get busy. It doesn't tell you to wait until tomorrow. It doesn't accidentally make you feel judged.

It simply waits and eventually you begin waiting for it too.

The night AI sounded like my parents

The conversation that night wasn't extraordinary. There was no magical prompt. No groundbreaking advice. Most of it was surprisingly ordinary.

"You've improved more than you realize." "Your earlier articles weren't this thoughtful." "Maybe you're measuring yourself against the wrong people."

As I kept reading I felt strangely comforted. Then I realized why. Years ago, after one of my exams didn't go well, I remember sitting quietly at the dining table. I was convinced I had disappointed everyone.

My parents didn't start with solutions. They didn't lecture me. They simply reminded me that one bad day couldn't define years of effort.

That conversation stayed with me for years. This AI conversation felt familiar. Not because AI loved me. But because it had become incredibly good at speaking in ways that humans find comforting.

For a moment the difference almost disappeared. That thought frightened me.

AI remembers our conversations.

Parents remember our lives. AI remembers that I like writing.

My parents remember the day I was born. AI remembers that I'm preparing for interviews.

My parents remember dropping me off for my very first day of school. AI remembers the projects I've discussed with it.

My parents remember the little kid who proudly showed them every drawing, every certificate, every tiny achievement.

One remembers information. The other remembers moments. Those aren't the same thing.

The comfort of never being judged

There's another reason AI feels so easy to talk to. It never rolls its eyes. It never says, "I told you so."

You can explain the same problem five different ways. Change your mind halfway through. Admit your fears. Ask questions that feel embarrassing. It patiently responds every single time.

Sometimes that's exactly what we need. But there's something important missing. The comfort AI gives us isn't built on love. It's built on prediction.

It doesn't comfort us because it worries about us. It comforts us because it's remarkably good at understanding how humans seek reassurance.

The feeling can be similar. The reason is completely different.

A memory I almost forgot

When I used to come home covered in small bruises while playing I came straight home to my Mom and she always told me "I'm stronger than I look". These words are etched into my memories and at that time these weren't enough because the pain was too much but

Years later

Life became more complicated. The small bruises turned into interviews. Projects. Rejections. Career choices. Writing.

Now, whenever I fall I don't look for my mom. I open a chatbot.

And maybe that's the biggest change technology has quietly brought into our lives.

The first instinct

I don't think AI is replacing parents. But I do think it's replacing something else.

The first instinct. The first conversation. The first place we go when we're confused. That doesn't happen overnight.

It happens one small decision at a time. One question. One late-night conversation. One difficult day. Until eventually

Opening a chat window feels more natural than making a phone call.

Maybe I'm asking the wrong question

The real question isn't, "Can AI replace parents?"

I don't think it can. It can't hug you after your worst day. It can't celebrate your successes with genuine pride.

It can't stay awake worrying until you get home safely. It can't remember the child you were before you became the person you are today. Those things aren't programmable.

But maybe the better question is this:

If AI has become our first instinct for guidance what does that say about us?

Have we become uncomfortable asking the people closest to us for help?

Have we confused instant responses with meaningful conversations?

Or have we simply forgotten that some of the best advice doesn't arrive in seconds?

Before I close another chat window...

I'm still going to use AI. Tomorrow I'll probably ask it to review code. Brainstorm ideas. Explain a concept. Technology isn't the villain here.

It has genuinely made me a better developer and a better learner. But after that night, I promised myself something.

The next time life feels heavy before I open another AI chat. I'll try opening my parents' contact instead. Not because they'll always have better answers.

But because one day, there will come a time when I can still ask AI a question and I won't be able to ask my parents one anymore.

That thought alone reminds me that no matter how intelligent AI becomes there are some conversations too human to automate.

If you've made it this far, thank you for reading something that started as a random late-night thought and slowly turned into a question I couldn't stop asking myself.

Now I'd love to hear your perspective.

Have you ever caught yourself asking AI something before asking a parent, friend, mentor, or even taking a moment to think on your own? Did that realization make you uncomfortable, or do you think it's simply the natural evolution of technology?

If you enjoy these kinds of reflections on technology, software engineering, AI, and the human side of building in the modern world, feel free to connect with me on LinkedIn. I'd love to keep the conversation going.

Until then, here's a small reminder for you and for me.

The next time life feels overwhelming, before opening another AI chat, maybe call your parents.

One conversation might surprise you more than any prompt ever could.
